From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American lawyer

**Adrian William DeWind Sr.** (December 1, 1913 – August 7, 2009) was a tax attorney, political adviser, and founder of [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch").

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adrian_W._DeWind&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Adrian DeWind was born in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), USA, on December 1, 1913, to Norman and Ethel DeWind. His father was a [mechanical engineer](/wiki/Mechanical_engineer "Mechanical engineer"). DeWind attended [Grinnell College](/wiki/Grinnell_College "Grinnell College"), from where he graduated in 1934. He received his law degree from [Harvard Law School](/wiki/Harvard_Law_School "Harvard Law School") in 1937.

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adrian_W._DeWind&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Private practice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adrian_W._DeWind&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Private practice")]

After graduating from Harvard, DeWind joined the firm of Sage Gray Todd & Sims, where he began his practice in [tax law](/wiki/Tax_law "Tax law").

In 1942, at the behest of the prominent tax attorney [Randolph E. Paul](/wiki/Randolph_E._Paul "Randolph E. Paul"), DeWind joined the [Department of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") to help draft legislation financing the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") participation in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). From 1947 to 1948, he was Tax Legislative Counsel for the Department of the Treasury, before joining Paul’s firm, [Paul, Weiss, Rifkind, Wharton & Garrison](/wiki/Paul,_Weiss,_Rifkind,_Wharton_%26_Garrison "Paul, Weiss, Rifkind, Wharton & Garrison"), in 1948. DeWind worked there for 35 years, eventually heading the firm’s tax law department.

### Government service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adrian_W._DeWind&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Government service")]

DeWind was well known for his roles in government as a tax expert and policy adviser. He was on the tax policy commissions for [Presidents](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") and [Lyndon Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_Johnson "Lyndon Johnson"), and [New York State](/wiki/New_York_State "New York State") [Governors](/wiki/Governor_of_New_York "Governor of New York") [Hugh Carey](/wiki/Hugh_Carey "Hugh Carey") and [Mario M. Cuomo](/wiki/Mario_M._Cuomo "Mario M. Cuomo"), and was an adviser to the Commissioner of the [Internal Revenue Service](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service") and as Chief Counsel to the [House Ways and Means Committee](/wiki/House_Ways_and_Means_Committee "House Ways and Means Committee")’s Subcommittee on the Administration of the Internal Revenue Service. DeWind was also a political adviser to [Samuel Silverman](/wiki/Samuel_Silverman "Samuel Silverman") in his campaign for Manhattan Surrogate and to [United States Senator](/wiki/United_States_Senator "United States Senator") [Eugene McCarthy](/wiki/Eugene_McCarthy "Eugene McCarthy")’s 1968 campaign for President.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

### Advocacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adrian_W._DeWind&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Advocacy")]

Throughout his career, DeWind was a prominent supporter of liberal causes. In 1950, he began a long association with [NAACP Legal Defense Fund](/wiki/NAACP_Legal_Defense_Fund "NAACP Legal Defense Fund"), then under the leadership of [Thurgood Marshall](/wiki/Thurgood_Marshall "Thurgood Marshall"), advising the organization on tax law. He served on the boards of both the Legal Defense Fund and the [NAACP Educational Fund](/wiki/NAACP_Educational_Fund "NAACP Educational Fund").

From 1976 to 1978, he was president of the [New York City Bar Association](/wiki/New_York_City_Bar_Association "New York City Bar Association"), where he chaired a special task force on taxation for the [Municipal Assistance Corporation](/wiki/Municipal_Assistance_Corporation "Municipal Assistance Corporation") and made controversial recommendations on the revision of New York tax codes to prevent the exodus of businesses and individuals from the city. The bar under his presidency also recommended stricter and more transparent mechanisms for the oversight of the city’s budget and finances,[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In 1976, he served as a founding board member of [New York Lawyers for the Public Interest](/wiki/New_York_Lawyers_for_the_Public_Interest "New York Lawyers for the Public Interest"). From 1980 to 1992, he was chairman of the [Natural Resources Defense Council](/wiki/Natural_Resources_Defense_Council "Natural Resources Defense Council"). In 1986, he brokered an agreement between the Council and the [Soviet Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Soviet_Academy_of_Sciences "Soviet Academy of Sciences") under which American and Soviet scientists agreed to on-site monitoring of the conditions of underground [nuclear testing](/wiki/Nuclear_testing "Nuclear testing") in both countries. He was also on the board of the Lawyers Alliance for Nuclear Arms Control.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Dewind was also a founder of [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch"), and monitored the treatment of dissidents in a number of countries, including the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") and [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala"), where he wrote a report on government-sponsored violence against [Mayan](/wiki/Maya_people "Maya people") villagers.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adrian_W._DeWind&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Death")]

DeWind died at his home in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") on August 7, 2009. He was 95 years old.
